The 1-2-3-Formula

The Nightmare Story
This is not ficFon. It should be a true
story, although events can be out of sequence.
The sole purpose is to 	enter a state of rapport
with your prospect.

-- 4 of 24 --

3 States Of Your Prospect
When Someone Hits Your Sales Page
They Are In One or More of Three States:
Disbelief Mode
 	The way around disbelief is to build rapport.
 	Your prospect does believe one thing: You are be<er than
they are. Your job is to convince them that you are only
slightly be+er off because…
 	You’ll never enter rapport with someone who believes you
are too far above them. (The Expert’s Paradox)
 	The secret: You are an expert from necessity.

-- 5 of 24 --

3 States Of Your Prospect
When Someone Hits Your Sales Page
They Are In One or More of Three States:
Decision Mode
 	All website visitors are in decision mode.
 	Your goal: Move them from a state of decision to a state of
desire. This is done during these two stories, as well as The
Grand Offer.

-- 6 of 24 --

3 States Of Your Prospect
When Someone Hits Your Sales Page
They Are In One or More of Three States:
Discovery Mode
 	All website visitors want to discover something.
 	The first minute of your VSL must convey a feeling of future
discovery, assuming they watch un;l the end.
 	People love to feel as if they’ve discovered something new,
something exciFng, or something that answers a quesFon.
We will be using these terms frequently within your VSL.

-- 7 of 24 --

The Nightmare Story Formula
The Importance Of Anchoring Your Most
Powerful EmoKonal States To Those Of
The Reader
The Formula
 	The TransiFon (“I wasn’t always the person you see today.”)
 	The Intro (“It was a dark and stormy night…”)
 	The Bad Part
 	The Really Bad Part (The Focal Point)
 	The DeclaraFon of Independence
 	The Moral: Your Journey To Freedom

-- 8 of 24 --

The Nightmare Story Formula
The Intro
 	This begins the story of when you hit rock bo<om before
discovering what you’ll be sharing with the reader
 	Here’s how you create the story using The Specifics Strategy:
 	When was it?
o The date, down to the day and Fme.
 	What was the weather like?
o Rainy (“I was driving and I was scared I may crash…”)
o Sunny beauFful day (“everyone else was at the beach”…)
o Cloudy and Gloomy (“the weather reflected my depression…”)
 	What were you wearing?
o Two jackets (“because I was hiding my gut”)
o Clothes from K-­‐Mart (“not because I was a smart shopper, but
because I was a broke shopper…”

The Nightmare Story Formula
The Intro
 	Who were you with?
o “My skinny friend Lisa…”
o “All alone, as usual…”
o “My financial advisor, James…”
 	Where were you?
o The downtown office
o “In Greenbo, Alabama on the side of the road on I-­‐10…”
 	What were you feeling up un=l this moment?
o “I was excited because it was just my birthday…”
o “I was scared of being caught daydreaming again by my boss…”

The Nightmare Story Formula
The Bad Part
 	Begin by connec=ng a “related state”
o Connect to your reader emoFonally to build stronger rapport
o Related state: Anything posi;ve that reflects the readers
desires while seNng up the Bad Part
o Example: “At this point in my life I thought that things couldn’t
get worse... although they were about to and I’ll share that
with you in a moment... and perhaps you can relate to that
feeling and you know you need to change, too…”
 	The “One More Thing” Transi=on
o Example: “I knew I was in a posiFon where I needed to change
but one more thing had to happen before I really got it…”

The Nightmare Story Formula
The Really Bad Part (The Focal Point)
 	Intro To The Focal Point
o The event right before The Focal Point (the epicenter of your
rock-­‐bo6om story)
o Example: “Then Steve and I pulled into this convenience store.”
o Example: “I drove around by myself unFl I came across this
empty house.”
o In these examples, the Focal Point occurs within a convenience
store and an empty house.
 	The Transi=on
o “And then it happened:”
o “And then… I did it:”
o “And then my worst fears came true…”

My Example Nightmare Story
• 	I remember the day when I decided enough was enough. I’m going to share this story with
you now, even though it’s really embarrassing. I think you need to know how deep down the
rabbit hole I went before I discovered the stuff I’m going to share with you today, that allows
me to live this amazing life that I live right now.
• 	I remember it was late on a Tuesday night and my friend Steve and I were leaving the office,
and it was really cold outside. And the reason I remember that is going to be really clear in
just a moment.
• 	I walked outside and I got halfway to my car and I realized I leC my coat in my office. It was
cold enough to where I realized it was cold, but then it just crossed my mind. ‘It’s cold and I
barely feel cold, and is it because I’m so fat?’ I think it was. I was insulated by all this ugly
blubber.
• 	“I was thinking, ‘Oh, my God.’ It had already been a really rough day, and now I’m like,
‘Wow, I already feel really Fred, and it’s not even 7PM.’ I felt really crappy,’ as I always did.
But this day, it was really bad. Knowing that I had my own insulaFon just made me really feel
like crap. So, I didn’t want to go and get my coat, at this point. I just got in the car, and I
said, ‘Steve, drive me to the nearest convenience store. I want some donuts.’ That’s how I
was going to medicate myself—wolf down some donuts.

-- 16 of 24 --

My Example Nightmare Story
• 	We’re driving to a convenience store and it’s raining, and so the car is kind of sliding all the
way, and I’m kind of thinking to myself, ‘Maybe it’s because I’m so heavy, it’s weighted to
one side.’ I know that probably wasn’t true, but this is how I was feeling. (Again, noFce how
I’m emphasizing feeling.)
• 	We drove past a corner not too far from the convenience store, I noFced two prosFtutes
standing on the corner. I could tell by looking at them that they were probably and sadly
addicted to crack.
• 	I’m not being funny when I say this. I thought to myself, ‘I am addicted to a different kind of
crack. I’m addicted to sugar. Sugar is like my crack. I’m addicted. There’s no difference.’
• 	‘To top it all off, I’ve sold myself out.’ That’s what I started thinking. ‘I sold myself out. I’ve
become a food whore.’ Seriously, this is how bad it my addicFon to food and self-­‐hatred had
become.
• 	But, things were about to get a lot worse…

-- 17 of 24 --

My Example Nightmare Story
• 	I walked into the convenience store, grabbed my two boxes of doughnuts… roughly a night’s
snack at that Fme in my life… and proceeded to the checkout counter… when I noFced that I
had leC my wallet at the office. It was in my coat!
• 	In a split second, I decided to do something I never, ever thought I’d do:
• 	I stole those doughnuts.
• 	I hid them in my jeans… and the clerk didn’t even look twice at me because my jeans were so
big and baggy in the legs… yet Fght in the waist… so it was easy to slip those boxes right into
my underwear and hold them against my legs as I walked out of the store…
• 	In total shame, and with a feeling of absolute desperaFon, I wolfed down half a box during
the ride home.
• 	So, I guarantee you when I tell you I hit rock bo6om, my rock bo6om was pre6y frigging low.
• 	But you know what? That one day caused my enFre life to change. I vowed to read every
book and crawl under every rock if necessary to break my addicFon to sugar and lose my
excess weight, or die trying.
• 	The good news for you is this: Not only did I figure out how to drop the weight and keep it
off… I figured out how to enjoy my doughnuts while doing it. I’ll share my discovery with you
in just a moment… so KEEP WATCHING…

The Dream Story Formula
First, realize that your Dream Story can and
should be used throughout your VSL. We’ll
cover how later. For now, let’s just get it
wri<en down!
The Formula
 	Follow The Leader
 	The Specific You and The General They
 	The TransiFon Phrase
 	The Present Tense Promise

-- 19 of 24 --

The Dream Story Formula
Follow The Leader
 	A simple transiFon into the Dream Story that engages the
reader’s right brain (the creaFve mind.)
 	This temporarily disengages the decision mode and helps put
the reader into a desire mode.
Examples:
 	“Imagine this:”
 	“Picture this for a moment:”
 	“Picture your life…”
 	“Walk with me for a moment as we imagine…”

-- 20 of 24 --

The Dream Story Formula
The Specific You to the General They
 	Starts with you asking yourself this quesFon: “What is an
example of your number one USP benefit, and how does this
benefit play out in your life using a specific example?”
Examples:
 	“Waking up knowing that I’m going to eat pepperoni pizza for
dinner tonight with my favorite girl while we curl up and
watch a football game.”
 	“Driving my Ferrari to my private plane, as I take my hot
model girlfriend on a two-­‐week vacaFon to Maui.”
 	The Problem: These examples are your dreams. Do not
assume they are your reader’s dreams.

-- 21 of 24 --

The Dream Story Formula
The Specific You to the General They
 	The SoluKon: You must take this to a “general they”
statement: Something that any of your readers can relate to.
 	Examples using the two personal examples on the previous
slide:
 	“Waking up knowing that you’re going to eat your absolute
favorite food for dinner tonight…whatever that may be... and
maybe even enjoy it with your best friend!”
 	“You feel absolute financial freedom as you dart off to your
favorite vacaFon spot with your ideal travel partner…”

-- 22 of 24 --

The Dream Story Formula
The Transi;on Phrase
 	TransiFons the reader from the hypotheFcal to the
immediately applicable.
 	“You know what the greatest feeling you’ll have is?...”
 	“You know what the best part of this is?...”
 	“And the best part? You will…”
Followed by The Present Tense Promise:

-- 23 of 24 --

The Dream Story Formula
The Present Tense Promise
 	“Tonight, you are going to be able to walk into a restaurant…”
 	“Within a month, you will walk into any car dealership...”
 	“Tomorrow, you will go to any bar and...”
 This is a Powerful 	NLP Anchor.
 	Anchors the reader’s feeling to a specific day and =me
(Usually “today” or someFme very soon.) This creates an
urgency to act.
